mnemonics.title: DH Protocol Steps
mnemonics.content: A sends A=g^a mod p, B sends B=g^b mod p; Alice computes B^a mod p, Bob computes A^b mod p; Both get g^ab mod p.

mnemonics.title: DH Security Assumption
mnemonics.content: DLP Hard = Discrete Logarithm Problem is hard to solve in Z_p* for large prime p.

mnemonics.title: MITM Defense
mnemonics.content: Authenticate public values to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks.

acronyms.usage: The computational problem underlying Diffie-Hellman security
A stronger assumption used in cryptographic proofs involving distinguishing random from DH tuples
DH with fresh random keys per session providing forward secrecy
DH using elliptic curve groups for smaller key sizes
Attack where adversary intercepts and modifies communications between parties
Framework using certificates to authenticate public keys and prevent MITM

visualTips.visualization: Draw two columns - Alice (left) and Bob (right). Show: Private keys a,b at bottom; Public values g^a, g^b in middle; Shared secret g^ab at top connecting both. Arrow from Alice to Bob shows g^a passing through 'insecure channel'.

Draw three parties: Alice (left), Mallory (middle), Bob (right). Show Mallory intercepting g^a from Alice and replacing with g^x; similarly intercepting g^b from Bob and replacing with g^y. Mallory connects to both with separate shared secrets.

Visualize modular exponentiation as a 'one-way function' - easy to compute g^a mod p (forward), but hard to recover 'a' given g^a mod p (backward). Show this as an arrow pointing right (easy) and a broken arrow pointing left (hard).

analogies.analogy: Think of mixing paint colors: Alice has private color 'a' (red), Bob has private color 'b' (blue). They publicly mix their colors with a common base color (yellow). Alice mixes red+yellow, Bob mixes blue+yellow. Both can now add the other's private color to get the same final mixture, but an interceptor only sees mixed colors and cannot determine the original private colors.
The Discrete Logarithm Problem is like a one-way door - you can easily walk through (compute modular exponentiation) but cannot walk back (recover the exponent) without trying every possible key.

analogies.concept: Forward Secrecy as Burn After Reading
analogies.analogy: Ephemeral DH with fresh keys per session is like 'burn after reading' - each session's key is used once and destroyed. Even if someone finds the master key later, they cannot read past conversations.
